coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes presents a vital solution to mitigating environmental pollution caused by industrial effluents. This study explores the photocatalytic activity of metallo-porphyrins, specifically manganese (III) chloride 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrin (MnCl_TAPP) and copper (II) 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrin (CuTAPP), conjugated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) dye under visible light irradiation. The incorporation of GQDs with the metallo-porphyrins significantly enhanced their photocatalytic performance. Among the tested composites, MnCl_TAPP_GQDs exhibited the highest degradation efficiency and rate constant, outperforming CuTAPP_GQDs. The degradation efficiencies of RhB under various conditions photolysis, CuTAPP, MnCl_TAPP, CuTAPP_GQDs, and MnCl_TAPP_GQDs were recorded as 12.06 %, 41.09 %, 51.09 %, 66.06 %, and 81.15 %, respectively. These findings demonstrate that the MnCl_TAPP_GQDs composite holds great potential for visible-light-driven photocatalytic applications, offering a promising approach to environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic activity of MnCl and Cu 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrins conjugated to graphene quantum dots against Rhodamine B dye using visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes presents a vital solution to mitigating environmental pollution caused by industrial effluents. This study explores the photocatalytic activity of metallo-porphyrins, specifically manganese (III) chloride 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrin (MnCl_TAPP) and copper (II) 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrin (CuTAPP), conjugated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) dye under visible light irradiation. The incorporation of GQDs with the metallo-porphyrins significantly enhanced their photocatalytic performance. Among the tested composites, MnCl_TAPP_GQDs exhibited the highest degradation efficiency and rate constant, outperforming CuTAPP_GQDs. The degradation efficiencies of RhB under various conditions photolysis, CuTAPP, MnCl_TAPP, CuTAPP_GQDs, and MnCl_TAPP_GQDs were recorded as 12.06 %, 41.09 %, 51.09 %, 66.06 %, and 81.15 %, respectively. These findings demonstrate that the MnCl_TAPP_GQDs composite holds great potential for visible-light-driven photocatalytic applications, offering a promising approach to environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic heterocyclic dyes find extensive utility across various industries, including textiles, rubber, paper, plastics, and cosmetics. Moreover, they serve as prominent fluorescent water tracers [1]. However, the disposal of these dyes as waste into water bodies poses a significant environmental challenge, leading to water contamination. Due to their high toxicity and resistance to biodegradation, organic dyes represent a major pollutant in wastewater [2]. Notably, certain dyes, such as rhodamine B (RhB), are particularly concerning due to their carcinogenic and mutagenic properties. RhB, which is the focal point of this study, can induce harmful effects on living organisms, including skin, eye, and respiratory irritation in animals [3]. Given its hazardous nature, it is imperative to investigate systematic measures for the removal of RhB from aqueous environments.

The development of effective, low-cost, and reliable techniques for the removal of organic dyes from aqueous solutions is a hotspot of current research. In previous years, a wide range of conventional treatment methods such as adsorption [4,5], membrane separation [6], biological treatment, and coagulation have been developed for the removal of organic dyes in water [7,8]. However, the majority of these technologies are either ineffective, time-consuming, costly, or chemically intensive processes [9]. Currently, advanced oxidation processes (AOP) based on photocatalytic technology are the most widely used methods for the removal of dyes in water [10,11], owing to their easy operation, low cost, and high efficiency in decomposing pollutants into the less toxic H2O and CO2 [12].

In AOPs, light harvesting and subsequent electron transfer to generate reactive oxygen species (ROSs) in water are the main reasons for the efficient solar heat-harvesting properties of the photocatalysts. In most AOP's applications, semiconducting materials such as TiO2 and ZnO have been extensively used [13], and recently graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted much attention as a promising candidate for the development of a new functional supporting substrates [14,15]. Moreover, carbon-based composite photocatalysts are the new attractive class of zero-dimensional nanomaterials because they possess remarkable optical and electrical properties, low cost, chemical stability, high quantum yields, and high surface area [16–18]. The properties of GQD can be enhanced by chemical doping with heteroatoms and functionalization using organic and inorganic agents to effectively modulate their activity, therefore in this work GQD are decorated with porphyrins.

The modulation of GQDs properties through chemical doping with heteroatoms and functionalization using organic and inorganic agents effectively enhances their activity. Doping GQDs with non-metals such as nitrogen or making a composite with other semiconductors has received a lot of attention in water remediation [19,20]. Therefore, in this work GQDs are decorated with porphyrins to improve the photocatalytic activity of porphyrins [21].

Porphyrins shows an efficient sensitive effect on photodegradation of environmental pollutants due to their high absorption coefficient within the solar spectrum (400–450 nm, Soret band) and its good chemical stability in comparison with that of other dyes [22,23]. Porphyrins possess specific absorption, emissions, and charge transfer properties. Their ability to complex with most metals to form metal-conjugated porphyrins makes these compounds ideal sensitizers [24,25]. However, free base porphyrins are not ideal photocatalysts and to improve their applicability in water pollution remediation metalloporphyrins are preferred. The central metal of the porphyrin structure improves its optical properties, structural stability, and enhances its photocatalytic performance [26]. Therefore, metalation and the use of supports are promising strategies towards improving the overall quantum efficiency.

The porphyrin and GQDs possess hydroxyl groups on their surface which are expected to interact through hydrogen bonding to increase charge transport and separation for efficient formation of ROSs [26]. According to Ni et al. [27], the internal electronic structure and properties of composites are tremendously improved by incorporating GQDs. The fabrication of the GQDs related nanocomposites has been demonstrated to occur through the build-in electric field which results in broadening the spectral light absorption and impeding the inevitable recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes [28,29]. However, there are no studies on the metallated porphyrins and GQDs composites for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants which warrants further investigations. This is because the formation of bonds in photocatalytic nanomaterials hybrids has been reported as a promising strategy to provide electron transfer channels for designing highly stable and efficient photocatalytically active composites for water remediation [30,31]. Moreover, the functional groups on the surface of the catalysts are expected to possess high affinity with organic pollutants for efficient adsorption of the catalysts. The adsorption of organic pollutants on the catalyst active sites is a pre-requisite for efficient interaction with ROSs for efficient degradation and mineralization to H2O and CO2.

In this study, metallated porphyrins (MnCl_TAPP and CuTAPP, TAPP = tetra 4-aminophenyl porphyrin (Fig. 1 ) were conjugated with GQDs for the first time towards evaluating their photocatalytic efficacy. In a study done by Jokazi and colleagues [21] the synthesized MnCl_TAPP were used for electrocatalysis, and the CuTAPP was reported in a study by Prabhavathi et al. [24] where they covalently functionalised it with multi-walled carbon nanotubes. However, in this work, their photocatalysis properties were explored for the first time in the degradation efficiency of Rhodamine B dye in water. The results demonstrated that the conjugates of metallated catalysts performed better than metallated porphyrins making them ideal candidates for efficient remediation of organic pollutants, and this study is expected to encourage more investigations of metallated porphyrins with carbon-based supports.

All chemicals were used without purification and distilled water was used to make solutions, cleaning, and preparation of catalysts. N, N dimethyl formamide (DMF) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Merck. Rhodamine blue (RhB), ethanol (70 %), benzoquinone (BQ, ≥98 %), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.9 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich South Africa. Ethylene diaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, 99.0–101.0 %) was sourced from SAARCHEM chemicals, South Africa. MnCl and Cu 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrins have been synthesized previously [21,32].

FTIR analysis was done with PerkinElmer FTIR frontier 100 spectrometer and KBr method was employed to analyze the functional groups of the material. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used for hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of the nanoparticles and nanoconjugates; measurements were done using a Malvern Zetasizer Nanoseries, Nano-ZS90 (containing 633 nm helium neon laser), using disposable cuvettes. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx Eye Detector, using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å, nickel filter). Data was collected at various 2θ ranges (5–100o) scanning at 1° min−1 with a filter time constant of 2.5 s per step and a slit width of 6.0 mm. Samples were placed on a zero-background silicon wafer slide. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were treated using Eva (evaluation curve fitting) software. Baseline correction was performed on each diffraction pattern by subtracting a spline fitted to the curved background. SEM images were acquired from Zeiss Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) coupled with an Oxford EDX detector. Ground state electronic absorption spectra of the complexes were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. Quartz cells with 1 cm path-length were used. Aqualog HORIBA Jobin Yvon fluorescence spectrometer, and QTOF-HPLC-MS were used to follow degradation of pollutants to obtain absorbance, 3D Fluorescence Excitation Emission Matrix FEEM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was done using an AXIS Ultra DLD (supplied by Kratos Analytical) using Al (monochromatic) anode equipped with a charge neutralizer, the following parameters were used: the emission was 10 mA, the anode (HT) was 15 kV and the operating pressure below 5 × 10−9 Torr. A hybrid lens was used and resolution to acquire scans was at 160 eV pass energy in slot mode. The centre used for the scans was at 520 eV (width of 1205 eV) with steps at 1 eV and dwell time at 100 ms. The high-resolution scans were acquired using 80 eV pass energy in slot mode. A nonlinear least squares curve fitting procedure was used. A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MnCl and Cu 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrins
ce:section.ce:para: TAPP (75 mg, 0.111 mmol) was dissolved in DMF (50 mL) and heated at 120 °C, MnCl2 (139.87 mg, 1.111 mmol) was then added and continued to heat at 120 °C for 4 h. The mixture was then cooled down at room temperature and the product was extracted using rotavapor and washed with water to get rid of unreacted salt to give pure MnCl_TAPP. For CuTAPP (60 mg, 0.097 mmol) was mixed with excess of Cu(OAc)2 (177.27 mg, 0.976 mmol) in 50 mL of dimethylsulfoxide at 100 °C for 4 h. after reaction the solvent was removed by rotavapor, and a dry product was obtained. The product was then washed with water to remove excess Cu(OAc)2 from the product [33]. The structure of these porphyrins is shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of MnCl and Cu 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrins with Graphene quantum dots synthesis
Self-assembly MnCl and Cu 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrins to graphene quantum dots, Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para: The non-covalent coordination (π–π stacking) of GQDs with MnCl_TAPP and CuTAPP was achieved using a previously reported method [32] Scheme 1. Briefly, 2 mg of GQDs was combined with 1 mg of each MnCl_TAPP (0,0012 mmol) and CuTAPP (0,0013 mmol) separately in dry DMF (5 mL). The two mixtures were then subjected to ultrasonication for 2 h, followed by stirring in the dark for 48 h. Excess uncoordinated porphyrins and GQDs were removed by repeated ethanol washes and centrifugation until the supernatant became colourless. The resulting solid products were dried under vacuum overnight at 60 °C. Afterward, dry DMF (10 mL) was added to the dried solids and sonicated for 10 min, resulting in stable homogeneous dispersions that remained intact for several days. The delocalized π electrons facilitate the interaction between GQDs and porphyrins through non-covalent π–π stacking.

The photodegradation of the synthesized materials was contrasted with a reactor that we used previously [34]. The reactor uses LED lights as a low power energy and source of visible light. The adsorption-desorption equilibrium was attained by performing the experiments in the dark for 30 min. Then light was turned on and the degradation reaction was allowed 210 min in which samples were taken every 30 min to evaluate the degradation efficiency (DE) using the relationship that absorbance is directly proportion to concentration. The degradation was monitored by recording the spectra of the RhB from 200 to 800 nm range and changes of absorbance at 554 nm were used in to find the degradation efficiency (Eq. 1) while the corresponding pseudo-first-order reaction rates were determined by Eq. 2, where C and C0 are the absorbances at any time t and initial time t = 0, respectively. The experiments were conducted in triplicates.

The sampled aliquots were filtered using a 0.45 μm PVDF (poly (vinylidene fluoride)) membrane filters and the absorbance of the sampled aliquots was measured using a PerkinElmer UV–Vis Lambda 650S spectrometer and water as the reference. Moreover, the 3D Fluorescent Excitation Emission Spectra of the sampled aliquots were measured with an Aqualog HORIBA Jobin Yvon fluorescence spectrometer while TOC (total organic carbon) was measured with TELEDYNE TEKMAR TOC fusion to evaluate the rate of mineralization of photodegradation by-products.

Electronic structures of the diamagnetic porphyrins contain an overlapping π-framework which can result in complex and frequent electronic transitions upon excitation [35]. These electronic transitions are often overlapping and can appear as a single signal in other commonly employed techniques. To approach the prospect of elucidating complex electronic structures, magnetic circular dichroism (MCD) can be employed.

MCD is the differential absorption of left and right circularly polarized light induced in a sample by strong magnetic field oriented parallel to the direction of light propagation [36]. Fig. 2 shows the UV–visible absorption and MCD spectra of photocatalysts CuTAPP (Fig. 2a) and CuTAPP_GQDs (Fig. 2b) as examples. The A-term describes the breaking of degeneracies of the ground state and the excited state of the molecule as observed in Fig. 2. MCD spectroscopy was used to identify the main electronic Q(0,0) and B(0,0) bands of porphyrinoids, due to the presence of intense Faraday A1 terms or coupled pairs of oppositely-signed Faraday B0 terms as observed on the spectra.

UV–Vis spectroscopy was employed to characterize the synthesis of metal porphyrins, GQDs, and their conjugates in Fig. 2c. The absorption spectrum of the free base porphyrin displayed a Soret band at 428 nm and four Q bands at 515 nm, 559 nm, 597 nm, and 644 nm [37]. After metalation, MnCl_TAPP exhibited a red shift in the Soret band, shifting to 479 nm, and its Q bands merged into two peaks at 593 nm and 644 nm. Additionally, charge transfer peaks were observed at 443 nm, 388 nm, and 350 nm [38]. CuTAPP, a copper-metalated porphyrin, showed a Soret band at 419 nm and a Q band at 539 nm, characteristic of Cu-based porphyrin, as compared to MnCl_TAPP the Soret peak shifted to the shorter wavelength due to metal dπ (dxz and dyz) to porphyrin π* back bonding [39].

The synthesized GQDs displayed a strong absorption peak at 334 nm, as expected [40]. When conjugated with MnCl_TAPP, the MnCl_TAPP_GQDs showed a slight blue shift, with the Soret band shifting to 477 nm and the Q band appearing at 635 nm, along with charge transfer bands at 428 nm and 383 nm. This shift can be attributed to the interaction between the GQDs and the metal porphyrin [41]. In contrast, CuTAPP_GQDs did not exhibit any shift compared to CuTAPP as previously CuTAPP was conjugated to multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) by Prabhavathi et.al [25] and there was no significant change on the soret band of CuTAPP.

The hydrodynamic sizes of GQDs, CuTAPP_GQDs, and MnCl_TAPP_GQDs were measured using Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), as shown in Fig. 2d. The hydrodynamic size of GQDs was found to be 4.19 nm, while CuTAPP_GQDs had a size of 15.7 nm and MnCl_TAPP_GQDs measured 28.2 nm. The hydrodynamic size of GQDs is consistent with typical values for GQDs [42,43]. The increase in size upon conjugation suggests the presence of the metal porphyrins, though the significant size growth may also indicate some degree of aggregation [21].

The XRD patterns shown in Fig. 3a correspond to GQDs, metal porphyrins, and their conjugates. The broad amorphous peak at 2θ = 20° for GQDs is characteristic [44] of graphene quantum dots, confirming their successful synthesis. It has been reported that the broad nature of the diffraction peak on the XRD pattern of the GQDs is attributed to the nanoscale size of the GQDs with only a few layers of graphene sheets [45] MnCl_TAPP exhibited amorphous peaks between 2ϑ = 10° and 2θ = 70°. Upon conjugating MnCl_TAPP with GQDs, the XRD pattern of MnCl_TAPP_GQDs displayed both the graphene peak and the amorphous peaks from the metal porphyrin, indicating successful interaction between the porphyrin and GQDs. A similar pattern was observed for CuTAPP_GQDs, where crystalline peaks corresponding to CuTAPP were present alongside the broad graphene peak, signifying the presence of both the metal porphyrin and GQDs. The amorphous MnCl-TAPP_GQDs composite could provide a high interaction between the individual materials as they are both amorphous. This strong interaction is expected to result in efficient charge transfer and enhanced photocatalytic activity.

The materials properties were investigated to understand the existing bond vibrations and functional groups. Fig. 3b) and c) shows the FTIR and Raman spectra of the synthesized materials, respectively. FTIR spectrum of MnCl_TAPP demonstrated symmetric and asymmetric NH2 vibrations at 3312 cm−1 and 3187 cm−1, respectively. The stretching peak at 1590 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrations of CC and CN. The 1558 cm−1 peak represents the bending vibration of the NH2 group [46]. GQDs revealed distinct peaks at 1674 cm−1 and 1397 cm−1, which are associated with the stretching of CO and CO bonds in carboxylic acid. Additionally, the peak at 3216 cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching, while the stretching of CH bonds is represented by the peak at 2918 cm−1 [47]. The peaks appearing at 1662 cm−1, 1598 cm−1, 1517 cm−1, 1403 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of MnCl_TAPP_GQDs are due to the loading and interaction efficiencies in the composite as they shifted and vary in intensity.

The Raman spectrum (Fig. 3c) of GQDs showed distinct peaks at 1286 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G bands. However, in the MnCl_TAPP_GQDs Raman spectrum, these peaks shifted to 1286 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, respectively [48]. This change in wavenumber and intensity indicates that the bonding of GQDs to the metal porphyrin was successful through π-π stacking.

XPS analysis was performed to study the elemental composition and the oxidation state of GQDs and MnCl-TAPP. Fig. 4a showed that the materials contained all expected elements of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, while MnCl-TAPP also contained Mn 2p and Cl 2p. This demonstrated that the materials were successfully synthesized. When comparing the elements that appear in both materials, the C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s high resolution spectra is presented in Fig. 4b-d. The C 1s showed three peaks which were indexed to CC, C-NH2, and N-C-(N)2 functional groups at 282.55, 284.21, and 290.41 eV for MnCl-TAPP while for GQDs the peaks were observed at binding energies of 282.45, 283.21, and 286.50 eV, respectively [49]. According to Fan et al., the peak at around 290.41 eV can also be indexed to π-π* [50]. This demonstrates the difference in the chemical environment due to the different BE.

The O 1s spectra in Fig. 4c was deconvoluted to two peaks at BE of 529.83 and 531.77 eV in MnCl-TAPP and at 529.47 and 539.71 in GQDs, respectively. These peaks are assigned to O=C/O-C=O at low BE while the ones at high BE can be assigned to OC functional groups with the difference in the BE suggesting different chemical environments [51]. The same reasoning can be given to the observed difference in the BE of the high-resolution spectra of N 1s in Fig. 4d. However, the effect is very little in the N 1s spectra with two peaks at BE of 397.40 and 399.15 eV in MnCl-TAPP while the BE of the peaks in GQDs are 497.96 and 398. 66 eV which are related to C-N = and NC3, respectively [52]. When considering the BE of the high-resolution spectra (O 1s, C 1s, and N 1s), it can be inferred that the peaks in MnCl-TAPP had relatively high BE due to the effect of the electron withdrawing groups that it contains which affected the chemical environment around these elements.

The SEM images of the conjugates are shown in Fig. 5 . The CuTAPP (Fig. 5a) and CuTAPP_GQDs (Fig. 5b) as an example showed different shapes with agglomeration and aggregation for the conjugates and this was also observed for MnCl_TAPP_GQDs. The large rocky particles of CuTAPP of different shapes were improved by the introduction of GQDs in the composite. The size of the nanoparticles reduced and the observed reduction in particle size is expected to improve the available specific area resulting in more exposure of the photocatalytic active sites for interaction with the pollutants. The increased interaction would then ensure that efficient degradation of the materials occurred. In essence, the conjugation of GQDs to the CuTAPP and MnCl-TAPP is expected to show enhanced photocatalytic activity due to high surface area. Similar results were reported in literature [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) dye
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation of RhB dye were investigated with the synthesized nanocomposites and the initial conditions were (100 mL of 5 ppm dye solution, 20 mg of catalyst, 210 min with visible light). The degradation efficiencies of RhB with photolysis, CuTAPP, MnCl_TAPP, CuTAPP_GQDs and MnCl_TAPP_GQDs were 12.06 %, 41.09 %, 51.09 %, 66.06 % and 81.15 %, respectively (Fig. 6a) with corresponding pseudo-first order rate constants for photolysis, CuTAPP, MnCl_TAPP, CuTAPP_GQDs and MnCl_TAPP_GQDs of 0.000562 min−1, 0,00212 min−1, 0,00277 min−1, 0,00474 min−1 and 0,0069 min−1, respectively (Fig. 6b). The rate and degradation efficiency obtained with the metalated porphyrins of CuTAPP and MnCl_TAPP portrayed that MnCl_TAPP has a higher degradation efficiency despite the high crystallinity of CuTAPP compared to the amorphous MnCl_TAPP.

Interestingly, the incorporation of GQDs with the metallated porphyrins also enhanced their degradation efficacy. Previous reports [52,53] on the effect of porphyrins incorporation with GQDs has generally observed improvement in their optical, morphological and charge transfer dynamics as seen by UV–vis, SEM/TEM, and electrochemical properties/PL, respectively. Therefore, the incorporation of porphyrins and QDs resulted in a formation of composite with GQDs acting as a support or forming a heterojunction. MnCl_TAPP_GQDs composite gave the higher degradation and rate constant compared to the CuTAPP_GQDs composite. Interestingly, due to the low content of the central metal ion, its strong binding with the porphyrin macrocycles, and its incorporation to all organic GQDs is expected to minimize leaching of the central metal ion, resulting in low or no secondary pollution.

The high activity of MnCl-TAPP_GQDs is indexed to high light absorption of the material as demonstrated by UV–vis results. As the catalyst absorbs more light, more electrons and holes pairs are formed. The formed electrons and holes are then efficiently separated to be involved with the formation of ROSs for increased pollutant degradation. The adjustments of catalyst concentration have been reported to affect catalytic degradation of porphyrin based systems supported on carbon materials towards degradation of dyes and their optimization is important for each system [54,55]. Therefore, the best performing MnCl_TAPP_GQDs was selected, and the catalyst amount was reduced to 10 mg which enhanced the photocatalytic degradation of RhB with an efficiency of 97.92 % (Fig. 6c) with a corresponding rate constant of 0.01769 min−1 (Fig. 6c insert) which is three times higher than when 20 mg catalyst was used. When 10 mg catalyst is used, there is a very high reduction in concentration of RhB which occurs, followed by almost stable concentration of the degradation process until after 150 min which then steadily decreases with increasing time. To better comprehend this, the UV–Vis spectra of RhB degradation with time is shown which also supported the observed behaviour (Fig. 6d). The spectra of RhB before and after adsorption is similar with one major peak at characteristic maximum wavelength of 554 nm for RhB [56]. During the degradation process, there are emergent of two new peaks that appear between 300 nm and 390 nm while the second small peak appears between 450 and 480 nm. The formation of the new peaks affirms that RhB was changed into other products that absorbed at these wavelengths. Interestingly, all the new peaks first increased with increasing degradation time until 120 min to suggest more generation of these new products as intermediates. Thereafter a decrease with increasing degradation time after 120 min was observed to affirm that the stable region between 60 min to 120 min during RhB is associated with formation of intermediates first. Then from 150 min, the degradation starts to decrease as the intermediates starts to be degraded to other products resulting in high removal efficiency attained after 210 min.

The colour changes during the degradation process are shown by insert of Fig. 6d. The observations portrayed that adsorption only reduced the colour intensity of the solution while under light irradiation, there was change of colour to a completely different colour that is very faint to suggest that most RhB was removed to intermediates. The perceived colour intensity steadily increases until at 150 min after which it reduces to give a colourless solution after 210 min of irradiation which supports the preposition that intermediates form and they are further degraded in 210 min.

To better understand the spectral changes of RhB during degradation, the pH of solution was measured during every sampling time interval. After adsorption-desorption, the pH of the solution remained the same but further reduced to acidic medium during the photocatalytic degradation process (Fig. 7a). The sudden decrease in pH during the photodegradation process can be indexed to formation of intermediates products that have weak acidity which are only formed when the catalyst is irradiated with light energy. From literature, photocatalytic degradation of pollutants have been investigated and affirmed to occur through the formation of hydrogen peroxide H2O2 as an intermediate oxidant that can directly degrade pollutants or dissociate to form strongly oxidative and non-selective OH radicals [56–58]. While materials properties and pollutants play an important role, it is envisaged in this study that the formation of H2O2 acidifies the solution to below 6 where RhB is zwitterionic and may result in formation of dimers as influenced by its concentration [59,60].

The property changes are sudden and results in huge removal efficiency of RhB which is proposed to occur through formation of its dimers with multiple UV–Vis absorption peaks in the current medium. Due to the low oxidative power of H2O2, as it slowly degrades the dimers while new ones continue to be formed which result in a constant concentration of RhB until 150 min. As the H2O2 dissociates to OH•, the intermediate dimers are degraded to completion which was accompanied with a decrease in absorbance and colour intensity of the solution to clearness, but the solution pH remained almost constant in acidic medium. Therefore, it can be concluded that the pH changes are brought by formation of intermediate H2O2 that motivates dimerization of the RhB dye and the dissociation of H2O2 to results in complete degradation of the pollutants. The formation of H2O2 and its dissociation mechanisms has been explored in detail [31,61].

The photocatalyst demonstrated efficient degradation of RhB with high reaction rate constant that matched other photocatalysts involving RhB degradation, containing porphyrins, or supported on carbon based materials like GQDs from literature (Table 1 ). There are some few reports of the catalysts with a high degradation rate than the MnCl-TAPP_GQDs system. However, the comparison of results conducted under different geological, environmental, and experimental conditions is not a complete comparison that can give a conclusive discussion on which material performs better. Therefore, as seen from literature, comparisons of different materials are done under similar conditions to give valuable insights [62]. In essence, the MnCl-TAPP_GQDs is a promising catalyst for environmental remediation.

To prove the degradation process, the sampled aliquots were investigated with TOC and the results are summarized in Fig. 7b. The initial TOC reduced minimally after adsorption-desorption and further increased during the irradiation process to even above the initial value. As degradation progressed, TOC increased until it started to decrease from 90 min of irradiation. The final TOC removal became lower than the initial one and gave an overall efficiency of 42.7 %. The results also support the hypothesis that a lot of intermediates formed during the degradation process, increased the TOC concentration until finally the intermediates were degraded, albeit not completely as observed since only 42.7 % of TOC reduction occurred. The formed catalyst was within some reported TOC mineralization efficiencies obtained during degradation of dyes in literature [73,74].

Fluorescence excitation emission matrix (FEEM) has been used to map different EX-EM fluorescence changes observed during degradation. The pure EX-EM spectrum of RhB (Fig. 7c) shows three major regions within EM wavelength between 550 nm and 700 nm with different EX wavelength ranges of <300 nm (region 1), 300 nm – 450 nm (region 2) and > 450 nm (region 3). After irradiation with visible light for 30 min (Fig. 7d), region 3 becomes more intense while both regions 1 and 2 are split into new peaks within EM wavelengths 400 nm – 500 nm. The observed two new regions are indexed to the observed new absorption peaks in the UV region of the UV–Vis spectrum of RhB degradation and further affirms the proposed formation of new by-products that are modulated by the formation of intermediate H2O2 that acidifies the solution. As the degradation progresses from 60 min, the original regions 1, 2 and 3 fades in intensity as the newly formed matrices below regions 1 and 2 continue to increase in intensity (Fig. 7e). Original regions 1 and 2 completely disappeared after 120 min of degradation and region 3 continues to decrease in intensity while newly formed spectra below original EM wavelengths of region 1 and 2 increases steadily with increasing time (Fig. 7f). Remarkably, the newly formed spectra are the only one existing after 150 min which can be postulated to show that at this time, all the intermediates' products have formed and no RhB exists in solution (Fig. 7g). After 210 min, the clear solution (Fig. 7h) portrayed reduction in the intensity of the new peaks with a spectrum looking in likeness to humic substances observed which undoubtedly proved mineralization of the formed intermediates products. But a new peak is spotted in region 3 of pure RhB signalling the existence of residual RhB which together with humic like substances resulted in the observed low TOC removal efficiency. The degradation results corroborated the formation of intermediate H2O2 through irradiation of light on the MnCl_TAPP_GQDs system that changes the properties of RhB through dimerization and results in OH• induced degradation of the intermediates as H2O2 decomposes.

The photodegradation mechanism of photocatalytic systems is important as it results in understanding the generated reactive oxygen species (ROSs) that pioneer the mineralization of organic pollutants. In literature, the most commonly used method is the radical trapping experiments where scavengers of specific radicals are employed to portray the involvement of the radicals through the retardation of degradation rate when they are trapped [60,75,76]. The use of trapping experiments is also efficient for any pollutant under consideration with concentration of the scavengers as an important parameter that influences the observed results during trapping experiments. For accurate reporting, very little concentrations are employed to ensure that degradation inhibition occurs due to scavenging of radicals [77,78]. 3 mM concentrations of BQ, IPA, and EDTA were used as scavengers for O2 •–, OH•, and h+, respectively. EDTA resulted in a minor reduction in efficiency while IPA and BQ had considerable effect on the retardation of specific radicals towards RhB degradation with the effect highest when BQ was used (Fig. 8a).

The involvement of the ROSs generated by the MnCl_TAPP_GQDs follows the order of O2 •– > OH• > h+. The rate constants for BQ, IPA, EDTA and control are 0.00318, 0.00456, 0.01084 and 0.01769 min−1 (Fig. 8b). all the reaction rates in the presence of the scavenger decreased such that the highest reduction was observed for BQ of <2 times that of the control. In other reports, the formation of H2O2 is directly linked to the reaction between two electrons with appropriate CB (conduction band) energy (Eq. 3) or it may be formed during the reaction of water and oxygen with one CB electron (Eq. 4) [50,52].

Photocatalyst stability is an important measure to gauge the possible large-scale application of the synthesized nanomaterials, and it is normally investigated through re-using tests. The stability of the MnCl_TAPP_GQDs system towards degradation of RhB was investigated for three cycles with efficiency of 97.92, 89.56, and 69.24 with corresponding rate constants of 0.01769, 0.01432, and 0.00855 min−1 for cycles 1, 2 and 3, respectively (Fig. 8c). Evidently, the little reduction in RhB efficiency and the low reduction in the associated rate constant showed that the synthesized materials were stable when used within 3 cycles. After each re-use cycle, the catalyst was recovered through drying and the FTIR spectra was analyzed which showed that the ratio of peaks around 1400 cm−1 which are due to carbon functional groups changed as the catalyst was used for the second cycle before they merged to form one intense and broad peak. This is envisaged to occur as the RhB molecules and intermediates products accumulate on the surface of the catalyst (Fig. 8d). However, the general spectra of the composites were maintained to portray the stability of the synthesized catalysts during RhB degradation.

The mechanism of the photocatalytic degradation is an important part during the degradation of pollutants to deduce how charge formation, transportation, and separation occurs in the composite for efficient degradation of pollutants. Based on radical trapping experiments and literature [79], the photodegradation mechanism is deduced as presented in Fig. 9 . It has been reported that porphyrins can act as low-bandgap organic semiconductors to harvest light for ROSs generation [78,79]. In addition, metalation of porphyrins further improves their band structure and their oxidation potential depending on the central metal used [78]. The electronic structure of TAPP is reported with a band gap of around 1.8 eV through density functional theory studies [79,80]. The electrons in the LOMO of a porphyrin gets excited to the HUMO to result in electrons and hole pairs that form ROSs [49]. When the porphyrin is metallated, the metal provides empty d-orbitals that acts as the CB and may form degenerate orbitals with the HUMO of the freebase porphyrin resulting in improved optical structure of the metallated porphyrin semiconductor [81].

Upon light irradiation, the metallated porphyrin semiconductor electrons get excited form its VB to its CB to leave holes in the VB and electrons occupy the CB. The conjugation of the porphyrin with GQDs provides a separation mechanism that is induced by the formation of internal electric field such that electrons migrate from the CB of MnCl-TAPP to the surface of GQDs. This achieves spatial separation of electrons and holes and increased the lifetime of the photogenerated charges for efficient formation of ROSs [82]. Interestingly, the holes in the VB of MnCl-TAPP are not expected to possess enough oxidation potential for effective degradation of RhB. This further supports the little involvement of this ROSs during trapping experiments. Moreover, since the holes have low oxidation potential, they may not form OH• from direct oxidation of H2O and OH−. Since OH• formation and involvement was confirmed by trapping experiments, this may be due to dissociation of intermediate H2O2 as reported in literature [83].

The electrons play an important role in the degradation of pollutants through dual activity of (1) direct formation of O2 •– which was the main ROSs that was generated during the degradation of pollutants. (2) The one electron formation of H2O2 from H2O reduction as reported in literature [31,83]. The production of H2O2 in-situ and its decomposition to H2O2 results in the observed involvement and presence of OH• in the system. However the generation of intermediate H2O2 is affected by a lot of processes which affects its formation and dissociation mechanisms and it can be investigated further using methods reported by Malefane et al. [31]. Therefore, this discussion can be summarized by Eqs. 5–10.

RhB dyes are degraded via an oxidation mechanism that commences with an attack by free radicals on the dye molecule, followed by a reaction with intermediates then H2O and CO2 are formed as summarized by Fig. 9. Previous reports indicate that RhB dyes can undergo a complete mineralization, with no hazardous by-products being formed [84]. This is further supported by the observed TOC and 3D-FEEM results that the intermediate by-products were degraded to H2O and CO2. In summary, the metallated porphyrin excites electrons from its VB to its CB and the electrons in the CB are separated due to the conductive nature of the GQDs support to result in ROSs.

In conclusion, the successful synthesis of MnCl and Cu 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-aminophenyl) porphyrins was achieved, and they were conjugated to graphene quantum dots for the degradation of RhB. Based on the materials properties, the amorphous MnCl-TAPP and MnCl-TAPP_GQDs composites outperformed the crystalline CuTAPP and CuTAPP_GQDs with the rate of the conjugate of MnCl surpassing that of Cu by more than >20 %. The conjugation of GQDS decreased the average particle size of the metallated porphyrins and the high visible light harvesting capability of MnCl-TAPP_GQDs resulted in the observed degradation aptitude. RhB was degraded as demonstrated by TOC and 3D-FEEM and the degradation was initiated by the formation and involvement of O2 •– and OH• based on trapping experiments. The OH• radicals were postulated to occur through in-situ H2O2 formation and decomposition through a two electron transfer process. This work demonstrated the merits of metallated porphyrins that are conjugated to GQDs and is expected to arouse interest for in-depth design and investigations of this interesting materials for environmental pollution remediation.
